title: North Thoresby Lancaster Memorial
crew_summary: 8 killed including pilot Flg Off. Thomas H. Morgan, DFC
narrative: Lancaster ED583 took off from RAF Grimsby (Waltham) on 4 October 1943 on what should have been a routine aircraft test flight. All eight crew members had volunteered for the flight. Over the village of North Thoresby in the Lincolnshire Wolds, the aircraft suffered catastrophic structural failure and broke apart in mid-air. The wreckage fell in a south-easterly direction towards the neighbouring village of Grainsby. A large piece of fuselage crashed into the centre of North Thoresby, demolishing part of the village chapel. Remarkably, no civilians on the ground were killed despite debris striking buildings. Among the dead was F/O Thomas Morgan of the United States Army Air Forces, a 23-year-old exchange pilot gaining operational experience with RAF Bomber Command -- a reminder that Americans served and died alongside their British and Commonwealth allies in Bomber Command aircraft. The youngest was 19-year-old air bomber John Hill Rogers.

crew.name: Thomas H. Morgan
crew.fate_detail: Killed instantly when the Lancaster broke apart in mid-air over North Thoresby during an engine air test.
crew.bio: American exchange pilot serving with the USAAF, aged 23. Was gaining operational experience with RAF Bomber Command -- a reminder that Americans served and died alongside their British allies in Bomber Command aircraft.

crew.name: John Thomas Goozee
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the aircraft disintegrated in mid-air.
crew.bio: Warrant Officer serving with the RAFVR, aged 22. Second pilot aboard the aircraft.

crew.name: Ralph Frederick Worsey
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the aircraft broke apart. One of three pilots aboard for the test flight.
crew.bio: Sergeant serving with the RAFVR, aged 22.

crew.name: Charles Alexander Green
crew.bio: Flight engineer serving with the RAFVR, aged 20.

crew.name: Walter Edward James Giles
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the aircraft broke apart.
crew.bio: Flight engineer serving with the RAFVR.

crew.name: John Hill Rogers
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the aircraft broke apart. At 19, the youngest crew member.
crew.bio: Air bomber serving with the RAFVR, just 19 years old -- the youngest aboard. Born 1924.

crew.name: William Frederick Bristow
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the aircraft disintegrated over the village.
crew.bio: Wireless operator/air gunner serving with the RAFVR, aged 22.

cause_of_loss: Aircraft broke up in the air (structural failure)
memorial_details: The memorial stands on the village green in the centre of North Thoresby (The Square). It was organised primarily by David Barnett in conjunction with the Lincolnshire Aircraft Recovery Group (LARG). The village council is responsible for upkeep. The memorial is publicly accessible. Condition: good.

external_narratives.summary: Detailed account of Lancaster ED583 (HW-K) from 100 Squadron. The aircraft took off from RAF Grimsby at 1205 to air test the outer starboard engine. At approximately 1250 it broke up over North Thoresby, with debris demolishing the village chapel and damaging houses. F/O Thomas Morgan was a USAAF exchange pilot. All eight crew killed.
key_details: Took off at 1205 hrs for engine air test
Morgan was USAAF exchange pilot with DFC
external_narratives.full_narrative:
• Kracker Archive • Allied Losses • Archiwum Polish • Paradie Canadian • RCAF • RAAF • RNZAF • USA • Searchable Lists Add to or correct this story with a few clicks. Archive Report: Allied Forces Compiled from official National Archive and Service sources, contemporary press reports, personal logbooks, diaries and correspondence, reference books, other sources, and interviews. Check our Research databases: Database List . We seek additional information and photographs. Please contact us via the Helpdesk. 04.10.1943 100 Squadron Lancaster III ED583 HW:K FO. Thomas H. Morgan DFC Operation: Aircraft test flight

Date: 4th October 1943 (Monday)

Unit No: 100 Squadron, 1 Group, Bomber Command

Base: RAF Grimsby, Lincolnshire

Location: North Thoresby, Lincolnshire, England

Pilot: FO. Thomas Hurley ‘Tommy’ Morgan DFC, T-190798 USAAF Age 23. Killed (1)

Pilot: WO. John Thomas Goozee 552903 RAFVR Age 22. Killed

Pilot: Sgt. Ralph Frederick Worsey 1392269 RAFVR Age 22. Killed

Flt Eng: Sgt. Charles Alexander Green 1622553 RAFVR Age 20. Killed.

Flt Eng: Sgt. Walter Edward James Giles DFM, 1194020 RAFVR Age 22. Killed

Nav: Sgt. John Alexander Baker 1395957 RAF Age 34. Killed

Bomb Aimer: Sgt. John Hill Rogers 1545048 RAFVR Age 19. Killed (2)

WOp/Air Gnr: Sgt. William Frederick Bristow 1268417 RAFVR Age 22. Killed

Above left to right: FO. Morgan DFC, Sgt. Rogers and Sgt. Giles DFM.

Above Lancaster ED583 HW:K

ED583 took-off from RAF Grimsby at 12.05 hrs to air test the outer starboard engine and to gain as much height as possible. The crew, not unusually, volunteered to test the aircraft.

At approximately 12:50 hrs the aircraft suffered severe structural failure and broke up in the air over North Thoresby, 7 miles south of Cleethorpes, Lincolnshire. The final moments have been described as a steep dive, almost vertical, with no tail and outer portions of the wings missing.

Debris was scattered over a wide area with some of it destroying a chapel in the village and damaging a number of houses. Fortunately with no loss of life on the ground. All the 8 crew members were killed and are buried in various cemeteries within the United Kingdom, having been claimed by their next of kin.

(1) Tommy Morgan joined the RCAF training School in Brandon, Manitoba on the 15th April 1941. He was a Sergeant when he later transferred to the USAAF and eventually came to fly Lancaster's with 100 Squadron. At the end of May/early June 1943 he was promoted to Flight Officer (FO)

Note: Flight Officer (FO) equates to the rank of Warrant Officer (WO) in the RAF.

Around the time of his 27th mission over Germany he was awarded the USAAF Distinguished Flying Cross (DFC) and his Flt Eng., Sgt. Jim Giles, the Distinguished Flying Medal (DFM) (London Gazette 7th December 1943) for an exploit over Germany.

Above: Courtesy of The Province, dated 15th October 1943

Tommy had completed his operational tour and had written to his girlfriend Irene, only days before his death to say that he had finished with flying.

(2) Sgt. John Hill Rogers.

Above: Courtesy of the Chester Chronicle and Cheshire and North Wales General Advertiser, dated 9th October 1943

Memorial to the crew of ED583 at North Thoresby (Courtesy of Sally Jowitt)

FO. Thomas Hurley Morgan DFC. Cambridge American Cemetery, Block K, Row 7, Grave 15. Born on the 20th February 1920 in St. Helens, Oregan, USA. Son of Charles Dwain (deceased in September 1921) and Susan Winnifred (née Hurley) Morgan (remarried name Cahill) of Lillooet, British Columbia, Canada.

WO. John Thomas Goozee. Islington Cemetery and Crematorium, Sec. P. Block. 6, Grave 7427P. Born on the 8th August 1921 in Barnet, Greater London. Son of John Donovan and Mary Ann (née Flaherty) Goozee of Kingsbury. Husband of Doris May (née Green) Goozee, of Kenton, Harrow, England.

Sgt. Ralph Frederick Worsey. Woolwich Cemetery, Sec. 2. Grave 175. Born in the 3rd Qtr of 1921 in Plumstead, Greater London. Son of Frederick William John (deceased in November 1926) and Kate Louisa (née Barnes) Worsey of Plumstead, Greater London, England

Sgt. Charles Alexander Green. Hull Eastern Cemetery Compt. 169, Grave 75. Born on the 4th May 1923 in Sculcoates, Yorkshire. Son of Charles Alexander and Lillian (née Fletcher) Green of Hull, England.

Sgt. Walter Edward James Giles DFM. Oxford (Rose Hill) Cemetery, Sec. G.1. Grave 19. Born on the 20th October 1920 in Old Marston, Oxfordshire. Son of Walter John (deceased in November 1936) and Mary Evelyn (née Sawyer) Giles of New Marston, Oxford, England.

Sgt. John Alexander Baker. Lambeth (Tooting) Cemetery, Sec. 0.3. Coll. Grave 647, Screen Wall, Panel 1. Born on the 27th June 1909 in Kennington. Son of John and Minnie Elizabeth (née Bowden) Baker of Vauxhall, Greater London, England.

Sgt. John Hill Rogers. Upton (Holy Ascension) Churchyard, Upton-By-Chester Old ground. Grave 320. Son of Harry and Gladys R. Rogers of Chester, England.

external_narratives.summary: WMO entry for the North Thoresby memorials for Lancaster Bomber ED583, documenting the village green memorial and its condition.
IWM War Memorials Register entry for the Lancaster ED583 of 100 Squadron memorial plaque at North Thoresby.
Find a Grave memorial for Sgt John Hill Rogers (1924-1943), the youngest crew member at age 19, buried at Upton (Holy Ascension) Churchyard.
key_details: Buried at Upton Holy Ascension Churchyard

local_contacts.role: Local authority responsible for memorial upkeep
